html:
Kristian I, born in February 1426 and died on May 21, 1481, was King of Denmark (Danish: Christian 1 or Christiern 1) from 1448, of Norway from 1450, of Sweden from 1457–1464, and Duke of Schleswig and Count of Holstein from 1460. He was the son of Count Didrik "the Lucky" of Oldenburg and Hedvig of Holstein; he was also the nephew of Adolf VIII of Holstein.

Kristian married Dowager Queen Dorothea on October 26, 1450, and became the progenitor of the House of Oldenburg in Denmark. The branch that ruled Sweden from 1751 to 1818 was known as the Holstein-Gottorp dynasty, and he was also an ancestor of the Russian Imperial House, which reigned until 1917. He was therefore Gustav III of Sweden's great-great-great-great-grandfather.

== Biography ==

=== Background ===

Kristian was born in February 1426 in Oldenburg, northern Germany, as the eldest son of Count Didrik "the Lucky" of Oldenburg in his second marriage to Hedvig of Holstein. Through his father, he belonged to the House of Oldenburg, a noble family that had established itself in an area west of the Weser River in northwestern Germany since the 12th century. Based on the two fortresses of Oldenburg and Delmenhorst, the family gradually expanded its rule over the neighboring Frisian tribes in the region. Kristian's father was called "the Lucky" because he had reunited and expanded the family's territory.

After his father's death in 1440, Kristian inherited, along with his brothers Moritz and Gerhard, Oldenburg and Delmenhorst. He was also designated as the heir by his uncle Adolf VIII of Holstein, and, according to Adolf's wishes, he was proclaimed successor by the nobility of Schleswig. When Christopher of Bavaria died in January 1448, the Danish Council of State turned to Adolf and offered him the crown, but he declined and instead suggested Kristian. Previously (on June 28), Kristian had signed a declaration promising to uphold Duke Valdemar of Schleswig's pledge from 1326 (the otherwise unknown "Constitutio Valdemariana"), that the Duchy of Schleswig would never be united with the Kingdom of Denmark under the same ruler.

=== King of Denmark, Norway, and Sweden ===

Kristian was therefore elected on September 1st in Haderslev by the council of state, proclaimed on September 28th at the Viborg assembly, and crowned on October 28th, 1449, in Copenhagen. On the same day, he entered into a betrothal with Dorothea, the widow of Christopher of Bavaria; the wedding ceremony took place on October 26th, 1450. In southern Norway, Kristian was also elected in June 1449, while in the northern mountainous region, Karl Knutsson (Bonde) was elected, and crowned by the Archbishop of Trondheim on November 20th, 1449. However, on May 13th, 1450, 12 Danish and 12 Swedish council members agreed in Halmstad to renew the Kalmar Union, stipulating that whichever of the two kings lived longer would rule all three kingdoms, and that Karl Knutsson would cede Norway to Kristian, who was then crowned in Trondheim on August 2nd of the same year. Furthermore, on August 29th, the Danish and Norwegian councils reached an agreement in Bergen, known as the "Bergen Recess," stating that the two kingdoms should always be united under the same king (which they remained for the next 364 years).

However, Kristian was not satisfied with what he had gained. In 1451, he broke away from Sweden due to a dispute over which of the two countries Gotland would belong to. This led to a bloody war, in which Kristian lost the advantage he had previously achieved. In 1457, Kristian was summoned to Sweden after Karl Knutsson was expelled, and he was crowned King of Sweden in Uppsala on June 29th; his son, Hans, was also proclaimed as Kristian's successor on that day.

=== Duke of Schleswig and Count of Holstein === In 1459, Duke Adolf died, and Christian was elected by the nobility (in Ribe, on March 2, 1460) as Duke of Schleswig and Count of Holstein. In return, he had to issue the "landesprivilegien" ("territorial privileges") on March 5th, which later became the foundation for the Schleswig-Holstein views on the relationship between the duchies and the Danish kingdom. This was confirmed by the "tappere verbeteringe" ("braver improvement") in Kiel on April 4th. In these privileges, Christian promised to consider himself as an elected heir to both territories, not as King of Denmark, and he granted the inhabitants of both countries the right to choose one of his sons or legitimate heirs as ruler after his death. Furthermore, each country would have its own independent administration, and that both would remain undivided forever. Considering later events and more recent ideas, Christian and the Danish council have been strongly criticized for not maintaining Schleswig's status as a fiefdom under the Danish crown, but instead loosening the ties between Denmark and Schleswig to connect Holstein with the Kingdom. At the time, this was seen as prudent policy, as it was hoped that this would prevent the resumption of the long-standing wars with Holstein. From the German side, the people of Holstein were even accused of "becoming Danes." Christian particularly feared losing his newly acquired power in Sweden if he engaged in a conflict with Holstein, just like Erik of Pomerania had done.

=== Economic Problems and Rebellions === Until...

How certain are we about this?

For individuals from the medieval period, there are often contemporary written sources (letters, chronicles, church documents), so the main points are usually well-documented. However, these sources were mostly written by the Church and those in power, and they are unevenly preserved, which colors and limits the picture.
tldr: Kristian I was a powerful king who ruled over Denmark, Norway, and Sweden during the mid-15th century. He is known for having united these countries for a period of time and for his decisions regarding the borders between Denmark and the regions of Schleswig and Holstein.
lattlast:
Kristian was born in Germany and became King of Denmark in 1448. He was a very powerful leader who also ruled over Norway and later, Sweden. During his reign, the three countries were united in what is known as the Kalmar Union, meaning they shared the same king.

During his rule, he made several important agreements. He reached an agreement with the Norwegian and Danish leaders that their countries would always be governed by the same king. He was also involved in conflicts over territories, such as the island of Gotland, which led to wars before he was finally crowned King of Sweden in 1457.

Kristian also made decisions about how the borders between Denmark and the regions of Schleswig and Holstein would be defined. In order to gain control over these areas, he promised that they would be governed in a special way that gave them a certain degree of independence from the Danish crown. These agreements were very important for how the borders in Northern Europe looked for a long time afterward.
